monogr.title: The Orphan; or, The Unhappy Marriage;
A Tragedy, in Five Acts; By Thomas Otway. As Performed at the Theatre Royal, Covent Garden. Printed under the Authority of the Managers from the Prompt Book. With Remarks by Mrs. Inchbald.

quotation.p: All quotation marks and apostrophes have been typed on the U.S. keyboard.
Quotation marks that indicate block quotes have been silently eliminated.

hyphenation.p: Any dashes occurring in line breaks have been removed.
All hyphens have been typed on the U.S. keyboard.
em-dashes have been recorded originally using the —

normalization.p: Page numbers appear at the beginning of each page, no matter where originally placed.
Compositors' catchwords and running titles have been silently eliminated.
& has been used for the ampersand sign.
All other characters, those with accents, non-breaking spaces, etc., have been typed on the U.S. keyboard.
Writing in other hands appearing on these manuscripts has been indicated as such, the content recorded in brackets.

category.catDesc: author of main text and of works reviewed, if relevant, using LOC authority name headings.

list.item: Corrected titles in teiHeader to remove all items listed as NOT to be used in the Encoding Decisions, Header section, which I also updated to make more clear.
Added myself (with xml:id) as technical editor to the titleStmt

head.bibl:
Elizabeth Inchbald
REMARKS [on The Orphan]."Remarks." The Orphan; or, The Unhappy Marriage; A Tragedy, In Five Acts; By Thomas Otway. As Performed at the Theatre Royal, Covent Garden. Printed Under the Authority of the Managers From the Prompt Book. With Remarks by Mrs. Inchbald. London: Printed for Longman, Hurst, Rees, and Orme, Paternoster Row, pp. 3-6. The British Theatre; or, A Collection of Plays, Which Are Acted At the Theatres Royal, Drury Lane, Covent Garden, and Haymarket. Printed Under the Authority of the Managers from the Prompt Books. With Biographical and Critical Remarks, by Mrs. Inchbald. In Twenty-Five Volumes. Vol. XII. Orphan. Venice Preserved. Conscious Lovers. Revenge. Beggar's Opera. London: Printed for Longman, Hurst, Rees, and Orme, Paternoster Row. 1808. The first performance of this play was staged at the Dorset Garden Theatre in late February of 1680. Laura DeWitt and Mary A. Waters edited this essay for The Criticism Archive.
______

div.p: This tragedy was first acted in the year 1680, and, for a hundred years, had possession of the stage, and the hearts of the public.

There is a domestic interest in the fable, characters, and occurrences of the play, which forces attention and admiration; whilst it partakes of a certain horror, not perfectly consonant with delicacy, which forbids its final effect to be gratifying.

Dr. Johnson had just observed of Otway's "Orphan,"—"That it had pleased for near a century, through all the vicissitudes of dramatic fashion,"From Samuel Johnson's The Lives of the English Poets (1779, vol. I, p. 444). when fashion cast it aside. But there appears to be such a degree of good taste, and even good manners, in no longer giving countenance to its representation, that it is to be hoped, its present mode of treatment will never change.—Yet, some wholesome lessons will infallibly be learned in the perusal of this faulty work.

It is uncivil to say, to a whole dramatis personæ, that "they are all guilty of speaking falsehood;"Quote unidentified at present. b 2
and yet, excepting old Acasto, and his young daughter, Serina,Acasto is "a Nobleman retired from Court, and living privately in the Country," as described in the dramatis personæ of the 1680 edition. this may be said to every personage in the tragedy—therefore, it is proper it should be a tragedy, as such despicable conduct deserves exemplary punishment.

But the guilt of Castalio's falsehood is so ponderous, that the offences against truth, committed by his associates, are light in the balance with his duplicity—the wicked origin from whence came all subsequent deceit.The plot of the play revolves around Acasto's twin sons, Castalio and Polydore, both of whom are in love with Acasto's orphaned ward, Monimia. Although both brothers pledge to avoid deception in their pursuit of Monimia, Castalio becomes engaged to her in secret. While eavesdropping, Polydore learns that Castalio has been secretly courting Monimia, though he is unaware of their engagement. On Castalio and Monimia's wedding night, Polydore poses as his twin and enters Monimia's bedchamber. The next day, Monimia finds out that it was Polydore, not Castalio, with whom she consummated her marriage, and Polydore learns that Monimia is his brother's wife. Neither can bear the guilt—Polydore challenges Castalio to a duel, during which he purposefully runs into his brother's sword. After the duel, Monimia drinks a fatal dose of poison, and Castalio stabs himself.

Otway borrowed his plot from the history of Brandon, in a novel, called, "English Adventures."In his The Lives of the Poets of Great Britain and Ireland, Johnson writes that The Orphan was "founded on the History of Brandon, and a Novel called the English Adventurer" (1753, vol. II, p. 335). The history of Charles Brandon forms Part I of English adventures by a Person of Honour (1676), attributed to Roger Boyle, Earl of Orrery. After having chosen such a hazardous subject, few poets could have treated it even with decorum—none could have rendered it so pathetic.

In those parts of the drama, where the peculiar tendency of the story has not beguiled him into licentiousness, he is chiefly to blame, in having made Chamont so exactly that which Acasto calls him, "an ungrateful ruffian"Act IV, scene i. Chamont is a young soldier and Monimia's brother with whom Serina falls in love.—and for having made Serina so sudden in her love, and so unabashed in the repeated declaration of it, before all her friends. She appears even more amorous than idiots are generally supposed to be. She wants capacity for a foil, and must not be named with the charming Monimia.

Many objections have been made by the critics, to the improbability of that mistake of one brother for the other, which produces the most fatal event of the whole play—but amongst the mistakes of the self-same kind, which Shakspeare,Shakespeare's plays Comedy of Errors and The Twelfth Night center around sets of twins who masquerade as or are mistaken for one another. and a number of other dramatists, have introduced in their works, this, by
Otway, is far the most natural of any that has yet been invented.

The author's disappointment, both as a soldier, and a courtier, may be traced in most of his dramas.Otway joined the army in 1677 after learning that the woman with whom he was in love was having his patron's child. After failing to get soldier's pay, Otway returned to writing plays. He servilely flattered the court in his dedications, prologues, and epilogues; but when he spoke by the lips of his characters, then he spoke from his heart—And in the person of Pierre, in "Venice Preserved," as in Acasto here, he has breathed the spirit and the sufferings of poor Thomas Otway. Pierre is a soldier who has been betrayed by the country he served. Venice Preserved is Otway's second tragedy, first staged in 1682. Otway was known for his appeals to potential patrons in the dedications of his plays. However, the content of his plays often conveyed anger toward patrons and authority figures who betray those who rely on them.

He had been a cornet of horse, before he was an author, and had served with the army in Flanders;Otway's regiment was in Flanders in April of 1678. but, on some account, was cashiered. That his offence was not of a disgraceful nature, may be conceived by the following lines in an epilogue to one of his plays; wherein he treats the circumstance of his dismission, too boldly, and too lightly, to admit the suspicion, that any humiliating imputation was, in consequence, attached to his character.
But which amongst ye is there to be found,
Will take his three day's pawn, for fifty pound?
Or, now he is cashier'd, will fairly venture,
To give him ready money for's debenture?
Therefore, when he receiv'd the fatal doom,
This play came forth, in hopes his friends would come,
To help a poor, disbanded soldier home.From the Epilogue to Otway's Caius Marius (1679).

Dr. Johnson throws some doubt on the received opinion, that Otway was starved; or, rather, choked to death, by hastily devouring food, after long de-b 3
privation. But if this admirable poet did not perish from the immediate want, or eager swallowing of nourishment, it is certain, that his health and strength were wasted through poverty, and the difficulty of obtaining even the ordinary subsistence which cherishes life.In his The Lives of the Poets of Great Britain and Ireland: to the Time of Dean Swift, Theophilus Cibber recounts the tradition that Otway, after receiving a shilling while begging for bread on the side of the road, rushed to a bakery, purchased a roll, and choked to death as a result of attempting to eat it too quickly (1753, vol. II, pp. 333-334). In his The Lives of the English Poets; and a Criticism on their Works, Samuel Johnson does not deny Cibber's account of Otway's passing, but rather states, "all this, I hope, is not true" (1779, vol. I, p. 445). Although scholarship is divided on this specific issue, Otway's biographers agreed that he died in extreme poverty.

The author of "The Orphan"—a drama which melted every bosom with compassion for the sufferings of human nature, however deservedly inflicted—was driven to the lowest haunts of the poor, to shield him from the contempt, to which his necessitous state would have reduced him, from the rich.—Near the close of his days, it is allowed by all his biographers, he took shelter from this threatened scorn, in a little public house on Tower Hill,The distressed and unsanitary area surrounding the Tower of London was best known as a site of executions. where he expired—if not with hunger, or too violent an appetite in devouring a piece of bread which charity had bestowed,—with grief and pining abstinence.

This man, who is acknowledged to have painted the passions, especially the passion of love, more powerfully than any other dramatic author, was never in high esteem with his cotemporary writers. But Addison, who lived after him, and felt no prejudice from jealousy of a rival brother, has said of Otway— He has followed nature in the language of his tragedies, and, therefore, shines in the passionate parts, more than any of our English poets.From The Spectator No. 39, on April 14th, 1711.
